comments.text: The formula in [GrSpWi77] mentioned by Terry is very natural and one can fairly easily discover it for oneself. It starts with the observation that the problem naturally splits into a large number of disjoint subproblems, one for each number $x \le N$ divisible by neither $2$ nor $3$. It is also extremely practical for making rigorous estimates for the limit density (in particular, the limit density is computable). It reduces to doing some integer programming to find the values of $f(k)$. Using this approach I found that the limit density is $0.8009655909371?$ (where $?$ indicates that only the last digit is uncertain). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
The wrong upper bound quoted by StijnC is likely the result of [ChErGr02] miscalculating $f(45)$, the first counterexample to their "$f(k)=g(k)$" conjecture.
Very nice, thanks Sean! This (the decimal expansion of the limit you've calculated) would be a good addition to OEIS.

I had a minor bug in my program. The correct density is $0.80096575500655898909042032638808241...$ (corrected from the 7th decimal place onwards). It will appear in the OEIS here: https://oeis.org/A386439. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The following elementary bounds can be proven easily: $$\frac 45 \le \lim_{ N \to \infty} \frac{F(n)}{N} \le \frac 56$$ Take the complement of the integers in $$C=\cup_{i=1}^{\infty} ]N/6^i, 2N/6^i]$$ then every set of the form $\{n,2n,3n\}$ where $1\le n \le \frac N3$ has exactly one element in $C$, since $2N/6^i<n<3n \le N/6^{i-1}$ is impossible (which would be the only way to avoid the elements in $C$). In the other direction, as an improvement on the upper bound; the sets $\{n,2n,3n\}$ for n in $B=\cup_{i=1}^{\infty} ]N/6^i, 2N/6^i] \setminus \left( \cup_{i=1}^{\infty} ]N/6^i, 4N/(3*6^i)] \cap 2\mathbb Z \right)$ are disjoint. If two sets intersect, $\{n,2n,3n\} \cap \{n',2n',3n'\} \not= \emptyset$ for $n,n' \in B$, then $n,n' \in ]N/6^i, 2N/6^i]$ for some $i$ and $3n=2n'$, so $n$ is even and bounded by $2/3*2N/6^i.$ So this could not happen, so we had approximately $N/6$ disjoint triples.

The paper https://mathweb.ucsd.edu/~fan/wp/linear.pdf shows the limit is not $4/5$. The nested $[1/6^k,2/6^k)$ construction is mentioned explicitly (as the optimal answer for the same problem maximizing measure within $[0,1]$).

Thanks. So in summary: Theorem $2$ and $3$ in that reference thus imply the improvement $$0.8003194838 \le \lim_{ N \to \infty} \frac{F(n)}{N} \le 0.8009611$$ and conditionally on a conjecture the lower bound is approximately sharp.

To add some amusing color: the conjecture "$f(k)=g(k)$ for all $k$" is false, with the first counterexample being $k=45$. Confusingly, they say they computed $f(k)$ for $k\le 50$; unfortunately, "Table 2" only includes the values for $k\le 25$. The function $g(k)$ is very simple to compute (much simpler than $f(k)$), doable even by hand or in like a Google spreadsheet. I claim that $g(45)=15$ ("perfectly balanced" between the three classes). Meanwhile, $f(45)=14$, which can be checked at https://oeis.org/A094708 or by manually subtracting computing from https://oeis.org/A057561 . There is definitely room for someone to write a modern version of the paper with much tighter bounds on $\lim_{N\to\infty} \frac{F(n)}{N}$ given the improvements in computational power. But I don't personally see it determining the value in any concrete sense, nor determining whether it is rational.

In [GrSpWi77] the limit is shown to equal $\frac{1}{3} \sum_{k \in K} \frac{1}{d_k}$, where $d_1 < d_2 <\dots$ are the $3$-smooth numbers, $K$ is the set of $k$ for which $f(k) > f(k-1)$ (OEIS A004059), and $f(k)$ is the largest subset of $\{d_1,\dots,d_k\}$ that avoids $n,2n,3n$ (OEIS A057561). The authors speculate that $f(k) = 1 + \lfloor 2k/3 \rfloor$ if $k \neq 0 \hbox{ mod } 3$, and that an extremizer for $f(k)$ can always be chosen to be in a coset of $\langle 2^3, 2 \times 3 \rangle$ (this could be a typo, it seems more reasonable to conjecture that the extremizer *avoids* such a coset). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
